event: Anatomically modern humans inhabit the Balangoda region, leaving the earliest evidence of human settlement in Sri Lanka (the Balangoda Man).
Early Iron Age settlements with megalithic burials and proto-urban centers develop, notably at Anuradhapura.
Traditional date of the legendary arrival of Prince Vijaya and his followers, marking the founding of Sinhalese kingship according to chronicles.
Pandukabhaya founds Anuradhapura as the capital, establishing the long-lived Anuradhapura Kingdom.
Buddhism is introduced to the island when the monk Mahinda, sent by Emperor Ashoka, converts King Devanampiya Tissa.
The sacred Bodhi tree sapling is brought by Sanghamitta and planted at Anuradhapura, becoming a revered Buddhist relic.
The Chola prince Elara seizes Anuradhapura and rules over the northern plains for several decades.
King Dutugemunu defeats Elara and unifies the island under Sinhalese rule, a celebrated event in national tradition.
King Valagamba returns to power and oversees the writing down of the Pali Buddhist canon (Tripitaka) at the Aluvihare temple.
Queen Anula reigns at Anuradhapura, one of the earliest recorded female heads of state in Asia.
The Tooth Relic of the Buddha is brought to the island, becoming the supreme palladium of Sinhalese kingship.
The Chinese pilgrim Faxian visits the island and records detailed accounts of its flourishing Buddhist civilization.
King Kasyapa builds the fortress and palace complex atop Sigiriya rock, renowned for its frescoes and water gardens.
The Chola Empire under Rajaraja I invades and annexes the north, sacking Anuradhapura and ending its long reign as capital.
Vijayabahu I expels the Cholas and reunifies the island, establishing Polonnaruwa as the new royal capital.
Parakramabahu I begins his reign, ushering in Polonnaruwa's golden age of vast irrigation works and Buddhist monuments.
Magha of Kalinga invades and devastates Polonnaruwa, triggering the drift of Sinhalese power to the southwest.
The Kingdom of Jaffna emerges as an independent Tamil polity in the northern peninsula.
The traveler Ibn Battuta visits the island, describing its pearl fisheries, cinnamon, and Adam's Peak pilgrimage.
Chinese admiral Zheng He intervenes in local politics, capturing King Alakeshvara and taking him to China.
Parakramabahu VI, ruling from Kotte, briefly reunifies most of the island, the last Sinhalese monarch to do so.
Portuguese explorer Lourenco de Almeida lands at Colombo, beginning European involvement on the island.
The Kingdom of Kotte passes to Portuguese control, extending their dominance over the coastal lowlands.
The Portuguese conquer the Kingdom of Jaffna, ending the independent Tamil kingdom in the north.
The Kingdom of Kandy allies with the Dutch East India Company to expel the Portuguese.
The Dutch complete their conquest of Portuguese coastal possessions, taking control of the island's maritime provinces.
The British capture the Dutch coastal territories during the Napoleonic Wars.
Ceylon becomes a British Crown Colony under the Treaty of Amiens.
The Kandyan Convention ends the Kingdom of Kandy, placing the entire island under British rule for the first time.
The Uva Rebellion against British rule is suppressed, followed by harsh reprisals in the central highlands.
The Colebrooke-Cameron reforms unify the administration and open the economy, paving the way for plantation agriculture.
Commercial tea cultivation begins, transforming the highland economy after coffee blight, with Tamil labor brought from India.
The Donoughmore Constitution introduces universal adult suffrage, the first in Asia under colonial rule.
Ceylon gains independence from Britain on 4 February as a Dominion within the Commonwealth.
Ceylon is admitted to the United Nations as a member state.
The Sinhala Only Act makes Sinhala the sole official language, intensifying ethnic tensions with the Tamil minority.
Prime Minister S.W.R.D. Bandaranaike is assassinated by a Buddhist monk.
Sirimavo Bandaranaike becomes the world's first elected female head of government.
A Marxist JVP youth insurrection is crushed by government forces.
The country becomes a republic and adopts the name Sri Lanka, leaving Dominion status.
Anti-Tamil riots known as Black July ignite the full-scale Sri Lankan Civil War with the LTTE (Tamil Tigers).
The Indo-Sri Lanka Accord brings an Indian Peacekeeping Force to the island amid the ongoing conflict.
President Ranasinghe Premadasa is assassinated in a suicide bombing.
The Indian Ocean tsunami devastates the coastlines, killing more than 30,000 people.
Government forces defeat the LTTE and kill its leader, ending the 26-year civil war.
Coordinated Easter Sunday suicide bombings kill more than 250 people at churches and hotels.
A severe economic crisis sparks mass protests that force President Gotabaya Rajapaksa to flee and resign.
